text.heading: Missing Adedibu in Ibadan
text.p: When the elephant fell in Ibadan last year, the forest shook and the people knew that the man many of them love to hate is gone. Today marks the first anniversary of the death of Lamidi Ariyibi Adedibu, the man who was popularly referred to as the strong man of Ibadan politics. In the politics of Ibadan, Adedibu was the alpha and omega. He made and unmade politicians. Those interested in political office knew that it was in their own interest to court Adedibu. To cultivate Adedibu is not an easy task. For someone who had virtually turned himself into an idol those who curried his favour came to him on bent knees.

Since an idol is not a mere mortal like the rest of us, they also brought along the necessary items for appeasing Adedibu, who was at his best dispensing political favours. In Ibadan, the fear of Adedibu was the beginning of wisdom. He was larger than life and everybody including those who held power in Oyo State deferred to him. Why won’t they defer to him? Was he not the kingmaker; the power behind the throne?

Because he single-handedly installed governors, local government chairmen, councillors and commissioners, Adedibu was Ibadan and Ibadan was Adedibu. As the indigenes would put it, Adedibu kan, Ibadan kan. His home in Molete was a mecca of sorts for all manner of people – the high and mighty, the low and unlettered, widows and political jobbers. They were always in Molete to pay homage to the master. Even though Ibadan has an Oba under whom Adedibu served as a chief, his influence surpassed that of the monarch. He was law unto himself and as such he broke the laws of the land with impunity.

What made Adedibu tick was apparently the political influence he wielded in his lifetime. He was a grassroots man who understood the poor very well. He felt their pangs and pains. Since the lowly saw him as their benefactor, they thronged his residence. Afin Molete as his residence was called was always thick with people, who came calling anytime of the day. Come rain, come shine, they were there. They ate and slept in Adedibu’s house. Adedibu made them feel important by eating from the same bowl with them. He shared almost everything he had with them. Through his generosity and kindness, he bought their loyalty.

This was why these ‘boys’ were ready to do anything for the apostle of amala politics. When Baba as they called him died on June 11, last year, something also died in them. A part of them died because they knew that there would no longer be freebies for them. Besides, politicians would now be using and dumping them. Unlike Adedibu who used and shielded them. One year after Adedibu’s passage, Ibadan seems to be missing him and his troubles.

Trust Adedibu. If he was alive today, he may be spearheading the second term campaign for President Umar Yar’Adua in defiance of the President’s directive stopping such campaign. By now, Adedibu would have set up shop in Ibadan and mobilised his ‘boys’ to go about town rooting for Yar’Adua. In the process, Adedibu would not give a hoot if heads and limbs are broken. All that matters to him would be that Ibadan, nay Oyo State, supports Yar’Adua. By now too, he would be working to get a second term ticket for his political godson and acolyte Governor Adebayo Alao-Akala. Alao-Akala was totally loyal to Adedibu in his lifetime. He used the master’s political structure to get to office in 2007. With Adedibu’s death, Alao-Alaka seems to have come of age politically. He is ready to run on his own steam in 2011.

Since Adedibu’s death, Alao-Akala has built his own political structure. He is now the undisputable leader of the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) in Oyo State. With his new found power, he has become a threat to some people, among them those close to his late political godfather. The political ambition of these people is hanging in the balance, all because of their differences with Alao-Akala. See what death has caused! With Adedibu’s passage, the terrain now belongs to Alao-Akala.
text.heading: The June 12 saga
text.p: As a friend to many generals, the late Chief M. K.O. Abiola was expected to know the mindset of the military. Abiola probably thought he knew how military men think, and this perhaps informed his decision to contest the June 12, 1993 presidential election. Abiola was not new to politics when he decided to go for the nation’s plum job 16 years ago. He had tried and burnt his fingers in politics in the Second Republic. His wife, the late Simbiat, also ran for the Senate and lost.

After that experience, Abiola and his wife quit politics and faced their business squarely. To manage the chains of business owned by the Abiolas is not easy, not to talk of mixing it with politics. Abiola quit politics then because the defunct National Party of Nigeria (NPN) to which he belonged felt he could not aspire to lead it. Abiola had wanted to be the party’s chairman, but the Northerners who were in the majority in NPN thought otherwise. They told him unequivocally that the chairmanship was not for sale, meaning that with all his wealth, he could not head the party.

Abiola felt slighted because he had done much for the party and also used his then flourishing newspaper, National Concord, to fight the defunct Unity Party of Nigeria (UPN) and its leader, the late Chief Obafemi Awolowo, who was and still is highly-revered in Yorubaland. By taking on Awo, many Yoruba indigenes believe, Abiola committed sacrilege. And for this, they were not prepared to forgive him politically. If not for his large-heartedness and legendary generosity, what his people considered his misdemeanour would have affected his chances at the polls in 1993.

But because of his nature and the forgiving spirit of his people, Abiola pulled through. He emerged winner of the June 12, 1993 presidential election, which has been described as the freest and fairest ever held in the country. But the military, or better still the friend he thought he knew so well – General Ibrahim Babangida - was waiting in the wings to scuttle his victory. Till today, Babangida has not been able to tell Nigerians why he annulled the election. Whenever he has the opportunity to tell the nation why he took that action, he skirts around the issue, preferring to talk from both sides of the mouth.

For how long will Babangida continue to take us for a ride? Will he ever come clean with the nation on why he annulled the June 12 election? Why is he afraid of a full disclosure? Is he no longer the general who dominates his environment? Whether he talks now or in the future, some day, somehow, the truth about the June 12 saga will be told.
text.heading: Adieu, the people’s chair
text.p: The news of the death of Alhaji Ladi Lawal in the early hours of Saturday, May 23 came to me through a text message from my colleague, Mrs Dupe Olaoye-Osinkolu popularly known as Mama Labour. Until his death, I never knew that "my chair" as we used to call him those days was ill. It has been long since I saw him except when I occasionally watch him on television.

I knew Ladi Lawal when I was a rookie reporter with The PunchNewspaper. He was then with the Federal Radio Corporation of Nigeria (FRCN) and was campaigning for the chairman of the Lagos State Council of the Nigeria Union of Journalists (NUJ). Baba Africa, yes, the one and only Baba Africa, Mr Dapo Aderinola, who was then the council chairman and our Chief News Editor in the The Punch, was rooting for Lawal who had served diligently as his secretary.
text.heading: Africa
text.p: got us all in The Punch to vote for Lawal and the rest, as they say is history. Lawal served two terms and crowned it with the president of our union in 1993. Lawal was a great unionist who never allowed his unionism to clash with his job. This was why the Federal Government found it difficult to sack him even when he was a thorn in its flesh at FRCN.

I learnt one thing from Lawal and that is: you can be a great unionist without compremising your job. The naming of the Lagos NUJ Press cCentre at Ikeja after him last Saturday is an honour well deserved. My chair, you made us proud as our leader. I thank God for your life but I thank you for giving us Mrs Funke Fadugba, the woman who elevated the status of many Lagos journalists.
